title: Detect cycle BFS
description: Detect cycle using BFS with parent

examples.explanation: Start at node 0: visited[0]=True, parent[0]=-1, queue=[0]. Process 0: neighbors [1]. 1 not visited: visited[1]=True, parent[1]=0, queue=[1]. Process 1: neighbors [0,2]. 0 is visited but is parent (0), skip. 2 not visited: visited[2]=True, parent[2]=1, queue=[2]. Process 2: neighbors [1,0]. 1 is visited but is parent (1), skip. 0 is visited AND not parent (parent[2]=1, so 0≠1): CYCLE DETECTED, return True.

Start at node 0: visited[0]=True, parent[0]=-1, queue=[0]. Process 0: neighbors [1]. 1 not visited: visited[1]=True, parent[1]=0, queue=[1]. Process 1: neighbors [0,2]. 0 is visited but is parent, skip. 2 not visited: visited[2]=True, parent[2]=1, queue=[2]. Process 2: neighbors [1,3]. 1 is visited but is parent, skip. 3 not visited: visited[3]=True, parent[3]=2, queue=[3]. Process 3: neighbors [2]. 2 is visited but is parent, skip. Queue empty, no cycle detected, return False.

approaches.name: Approach 1: BFS with Parent Tracking
approaches.summary: Use BFS to traverse the graph, tracking each node's parent to avoid revisiting it as a cycle. If a visited node is encountered that is not the parent, a cycle is detected.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Detect Cycle in Undirected Graph using BFS with Parent Tracking
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to detect cycles in an undirected graph using BFS with parent tracking.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires detecting whether an undirected graph contains a cycle. The BFS with parent tracking approach works by performing breadth-first search while maintaining a parent pointer for each node. The intuition is that in an undirected graph, during BFS traversal, if we encounter a visited node that is NOT the parent of the current node, we've found a back edge indicating a cycle. This works because in BFS, we explore nodes level by level, and any edge connecting to a previously visited node at the same or higher level (except the parent) creates a cycle. The algorithm starts by initializing visited arrays and parent pointers. For each unvisited node, we perform BFS using a queue. For each neighbor of the current node, if it's not visited, we mark it visited, set its parent, and enqueue it. If the neighbor is visited AND it's not the parent, we've detected a cycle. Time complexity is O(V+E) since we visit each node and edge once. Space complexity is O(V) for the queue, visited array, and parent array. This connects to L1 by using the BFS traversal subroutine. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle disconnected graphs, incorrectly checking parent condition, or using directed graph logic. Real code pattern: Initialize visited array, for each node if not visited: BFS with queue, for each neighbor: if not visited: mark visited, set parent, enqueue; else if neighbor != parent: return True.

examples.trace: Start at node 0: visited[0]=True, parent[0]=-1, queue=[0]. Process 0: neighbors [1]. 1 not visited: visited[1]=True, parent[1]=0, queue=[1]. Process 1: neighbors [0,2]. 0 is visited but is parent (0), skip. 2 not visited: visited[2]=True, parent[2]=1, queue=[2]. Process 2: neighbors [1,0]. 1 is visited but is parent (1), skip. 0 is visited AND not parent (parent[2]=1, so 0≠1): CYCLE DETECTED, return True.

Start at node 0: visited[0]=True, parent[0]=-1, queue=[0]. Process 0: neighbors [1]. 1 not visited: visited[1]=True, parent[1]=0, queue=[1]. Process 1: neighbors [0,2]. 0 is visited but is parent, skip. 2 not visited: visited[2]=True, parent[2]=1, queue=[2]. Process 2: neighbors [1,3]. 1 is visited but is parent, skip. 3 not visited: visited[3]=True, parent[3]=2, queue=[3]. Process 3: neighbors [2]. 2 is visited but is parent, skip. Queue empty, no cycle detected, return False.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — BFS Traversal with Parent Tracking for a Single Component
ladders.desc: Perform BFS on a starting node while tracking parent nodes to detect cycles within one connected component.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine implements BFS traversal from a given starting node while maintaining parent pointers to detect cycles within that connected component. The intuition is that BFS explores nodes layer by layer, and by tracking the parent (the node we came from), we can distinguish between legitimate tree edges (parent-child) and back edges that indicate cycles. The algorithm uses a queue to process nodes. For each node dequeued, we examine all its neighbors. If a neighbor is unvisited, we mark it visited, record its parent as the current node, and enqueue it. If a neighbor is already visited AND it's not the parent of the current node, we've found a cycle. This works because in an undirected graph, the only visited neighbor that should be encountered during BFS is the parent—any other visited neighbor means there's an alternative path, creating a cycle. Time complexity is O(V+E) for the component. Space complexity is O(V) for the queue and data structures. This feeds into L0 by being called for each unvisited node to handle disconnected graphs. Common mistakes include not initializing parent correctly, forgetting to skip the parent check, or mishandling the queue. Real code pattern: queue = [start], visited[start]=True, parent[start]=-1; while queue: node = queue.pop(0); for neighbor in graph[node]: if not visited[neighbor]: visited[neighbor]=True, parent[neighbor]=node, queue.append(neighbor); elif neighbor != parent[node]: return True; return False.

examples.trace: Start BFS at node 0: visited[0]=True, parent[0]=-1, queue=[0]. Process 0: neighbors [1]. 1 not visited: visited[1]=True, parent[1]=0, queue=[1]. Process 1: neighbors [0,2]. 0 is visited but is parent (0), skip. 2 not visited: visited[2]=True, parent[2]=1, queue=[2]. Process 2: neighbors [1,0]. 1 is visited but is parent (1), skip. 0 is visited AND not parent (parent[2]=1, so 0≠1): CYCLE DETECTED, return True.

Start BFS at node 0: visited[0]=True, parent[0]=-1, queue=[0]. Process 0: neighbors [1]. 1 not visited: visited[1]=True, parent[1]=0, queue=[1]. Process 1: neighbors [0,2]. 0 is visited but is parent, skip. 2 not visited: visited[2]=True, parent[2]=1, queue=[2]. Process 2: neighbors [1,3]. 1 is visited but is parent, skip. 3 not visited: visited[3]=True, parent[3]=2, queue=[3]. Process 3: neighbors [2]. 2 is visited but is parent, skip. Queue empty, no cycle, return False.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Detecting Back Edges in BFS Using Parent Comparison
ladders.desc: Identify when a visited neighbor in BFS indicates a cycle by checking if it's not the parent.
ladders.explanation: This core logic focuses on the key condition that detects cycles during BFS traversal: when processing a node's neighbors, if we encounter a visited neighbor that is NOT the parent of the current node, we've found a back edge indicating a cycle. The intuition is that in BFS of an undirected graph, edges form a tree (or forest). The parent pointer tracks the tree edge we came from. Any edge to a visited node that isn't the parent must be a cross edge or back edge, which in an undirected graph creates a cycle. This works because BFS explores nodes level by level, and any edge connecting to a node at the same or previous level (except the direct parent) creates an alternative path, forming a cycle. The algorithm step is simple: during BFS, for each neighbor of the current node, check if visited[neighbor] is True. If True, then check if neighbor != parent[current_node]. If both conditions hold, return True for cycle detection. Time complexity is O(1) per edge check. Space complexity is O(1) for the comparison. This feeds into L1 as the critical condition within the BFS loop. Common mistakes include forgetting to check the parent condition, incorrectly handling the initial node's parent, or applying this to directed graphs. Real code pattern: for neighbor in graph[node]: if visited[neighbor] and neighbor != parent[node]: return True.

examples.input: Current node: 2 with parent=1, neighbors: [1,0], visited: {0:True,1:True,2:True}
examples.trace: Process node 2 with parent[2]=1. Check neighbor 1: visited[1]=True, but neighbor (1) == parent[2] (1), so skip (not a cycle). Check neighbor 0: visited[0]=True, and neighbor (0) != parent[2] (1), so condition met: CYCLE DETECTED, return True.

examples.input: Current node: 3 with parent=2, neighbors: [2,4], visited: {0:True,1:True,2:True,3:True}
examples.trace: Process node 3 with parent[3]=2. Check neighbor 2: visited[2]=True, but neighbor (2) == parent[3] (2), so skip. Check neighbor 4: visited[4]=False (not visited), so skip. No cycle detected, continue BFS.

ladders.title: Building Block — BFS Traversal with Visited Tracking
ladders.desc: Implement standard BFS to traverse all nodes in a connected component, marking nodes as visited.
ladders.explanation: This building block implements standard Breadth-First Search (BFS) traversal for an undirected graph, focusing on visiting all nodes in a connected component while tracking visited nodes to avoid reprocessing. The intuition is that BFS explores nodes level by level, starting from a source node, which is essential for cycle detection as it ensures we encounter back edges correctly. The algorithm uses a queue to manage nodes to visit. We start by marking the source node as visited and enqueuing it. Then, while the queue is not empty, we dequeue a node, process it (e.g., check neighbors), and for each unvisited neighbor, mark it visited and enqueue it. This ensures each node is visited exactly once. Time complexity is O(V+E) for the component. Space complexity is O(V) for the queue and visited array. This feeds into L2 by providing the traversal framework where parent tracking and cycle detection can be added. Common mistakes include not marking nodes as visited when enqueuing (leading to duplicates), using DFS instead of BFS, or not handling empty graphs. Real code pattern: visited = [False]*V; queue = [start]; visited[start]=True; while queue: node = queue.pop(0); for neighbor in graph[node]: if not visited[neighbor]: visited[neighbor]=True; queue.append(neighbor).

examples.trace: Start at node 0: visited[0]=True, queue=[0]. Process 0: neighbors [1]. 1 not visited: visited[1]=True, queue=[1]. Process 1: neighbors [0,2]. 0 visited, skip. 2 not visited: visited[2]=True, queue=[2]. Process 2: neighbors [1,3]. 1 visited, skip. 3 not visited: visited[3]=True, queue=[3]. Process 3: neighbors [2]. 2 visited, skip. Queue empty, traversal complete. Visited nodes: 0,1,2,3.

Start at node 0: visited[0]=True, queue=[0]. Process 0: neighbors [1,2]. 1 not visited: visited[1]=True, queue=[1]. 2 not visited: visited[2]=True, queue=[1,2]. Process 1: neighbors [0,3]. 0 visited, skip. 3 not visited: visited[3]=True, queue=[2,3]. Process 2: neighbors [0]. 0 visited, skip. Process 3: neighbors [1]. 1 visited, skip. Queue empty, traversal complete. Visited nodes: 0,1,2,3.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Queue Management for BFS
ladders.desc: Implement queue operations (enqueue and dequeue) to manage nodes in BFS traversal.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation focuses on using a queue data structure to manage the order of node processing in BFS. The intuition is that BFS requires First-In-First-Out (FIFO) ordering to explore nodes level by level, which a queue provides perfectly. We enqueue nodes when they are discovered and dequeue them to process their neighbors. The algorithm involves initializing an empty queue, then repeatedly: dequeue a node (if queue not empty), process it, and enqueue its unvisited neighbors. This ensures nodes are processed in the order they were discovered, corresponding to increasing distances from the start. Time complexity is O(1) per enqueue/dequeue on average. Space complexity is O(V) for storing nodes in the queue. This feeds into L3 as the core mechanism for BFS traversal. Common mistakes include using a stack (LIFO) instead of a queue, not checking queue emptiness, or inefficient dequeue operations. Real code pattern: queue = []; queue.append(start) # enqueue; while queue: node = queue.pop(0) # dequeue; process node; for neighbor in graph[node]: if not visited[neighbor]: queue.append(neighbor).

examples.input: Start node: 0, enqueue 0, then process: neighbors [1,2]
examples.trace: Initial queue: []. Enqueue 0: queue = [0]. Dequeue 0: node = 0, queue = []. Process node 0: neighbors [1,2]. Enqueue 1: queue = [1]. Enqueue 2: queue = [1,2]. Final queue: [1,2].

examples.input: Start queue: [1,2], dequeue 1, neighbors [0,3] (0 visited, 3 unvisited)
examples.trace: Initial queue: [1,2]. Dequeue 1: node = 1, queue = [2]. Process node 1: neighbors [0,3]. 0 is visited, skip. 3 is unvisited, enqueue 3: queue = [2,3]. Final queue: [2,3].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graph Representation as Adjacency List
ladders.desc: Represent an undirected graph using an adjacency list, where each node has a list of its neighbors.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept involves representing an undirected graph using an adjacency list, which is essential for BFS traversal. An adjacency list is a data structure where each node in the graph is associated with a list of its adjacent nodes (neighbors). For an undirected graph, if there's an edge between node A and node B, then B appears in A's list and A appears in B's list. The intuition is that this representation efficiently stores graph connectivity, allowing quick access to a node's neighbors during traversal. It's space-efficient for sparse graphs (O(V+E)) and supports efficient neighbor iteration. To build it, initialize a list of empty lists for each node. For each edge (u,v), append v to u's list and u to v's list. Time complexity to build is O(E). Space complexity is O(V+E). This feeds into L4 as the input structure for BFS queue operations. Common mistakes include not adding both directions for undirected graphs, using incorrect indices, or not initializing the list properly. Real code pattern: V = number of nodes; graph = [[] for _ in range(V)]; for each edge (u,v): graph[u].append(v); graph[v].append(u).

examples.trace: Initialize graph for 3 nodes: [[], [], []]. Add edge 0-1: append 1 to graph[0]: graph[0]=[1]; append 0 to graph[1]: graph[1]=[0]. Add edge 1-2: append 2 to graph[1]: graph[1]=[0,2]; append 1 to graph[2]: graph[2]=[1]. Final: graph[0]=[1], graph[1]=[0,2], graph[2]=[1].

Initialize graph for 4 nodes: [[], [], [], []]. Add edge 0-1: append 1 to graph[0]: graph[0]=[1]; append 0 to graph[1]: graph[1]=[0]. Add edge 0-2: append 2 to graph[0]: graph[0]=[1,2]; append 0 to graph[2]: graph[2]=[0]. Add edge 1-3: append 3 to graph[1]: graph[1]=[0,3]; append 1 to graph[3]: graph[3]=[1]. Final: graph[0]=[1,2], graph[1]=[0,3], graph[2]=[0], graph[3]=[1].

approaches.name: Approach 2: DFS Recursive with Parent
approaches.summary: Perform DFS recursively, passing the parent node to avoid trivial cycles. Detect a cycle if a visited node is found that is not the parent during recursion.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Detect Cycle in Undirected Graph using DFS Recursive with Parent
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to detect cycles in an undirected graph using DFS recursion with parent tracking.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires determining whether an undirected graph contains a cycle. The DFS recursive approach with parent tracking works by performing depth-first traversal while keeping track of each node's parent to avoid mistaking the immediate back-edge to the parent as a cycle. The intuition is that in an undirected graph, a cycle exists if during DFS traversal, we encounter a visited node that is NOT the parent of the current node. This indicates there's an alternative path back to that node, forming a cycle. The algorithm starts DFS from each unvisited node to handle disconnected graphs. For each node, we mark it visited and recursively explore all adjacent nodes. If we find an adjacent node that's already visited AND it's not the parent, we've detected a cycle. The time complexity is O(V + E) since we visit each vertex and edge once. Space complexity is O(V) for the visited array and recursion stack depth. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete graph traversal framework around the core DFS detection logic. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle disconnected graphs, incorrectly checking parent condition, or using directed graph logic. Real code pattern: def hasCycle(graph): visited = [False]*V; for v in range(V): if not visited[v]: if dfs(v, -1): return True; return False; def dfs(node, parent): visited[node]=True; for neighbor in graph[node]: if not visited[neighbor]: if dfs(neighbor, node): return True; elif neighbor != parent: return True; return False.

examples.trace: Start at vertex 0 (unvisited). DFS(0, parent=-1): Mark 0 visited. Neighbors: 1 (unvisited) → DFS(1, parent=0): Mark 1 visited. Neighbors: 0 (visited, but parent=0, so ignore), 2 (unvisited) → DFS(2, parent=1): Mark 2 visited. Neighbors: 1 (visited, parent=1, ignore), 3 (unvisited) → DFS(3, parent=2): Mark 3 visited. Neighbors: 2 (visited, parent=2, ignore), 0 (visited AND neighbor=0 ≠ parent=2) → CYCLE DETECTED. Return True.

Start at vertex 0 (unvisited). DFS(0, parent=-1): Mark 0 visited. Neighbors: 1 (unvisited) → DFS(1, parent=0): Mark 1 visited. Neighbors: 0 (visited, parent=0, ignore), 2 (unvisited) → DFS(2, parent=1): Mark 2 visited. Neighbors: 1 (visited, parent=1, ignore). No cycle detected at any step. Return False.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS Recursive Function with Parent Parameter
ladders.desc: The recursive DFS function that detects cycles by checking visited nodes against parent.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the cycle detection algorithm. It performs depth-first traversal from a given node while receiving its parent as a parameter. The intuition is that during DFS in an undirected graph, each edge is traversed twice (once in each direction). The parent parameter helps distinguish between the legitimate back-edge to the immediate predecessor (which is not a cycle) and a back-edge to some other visited node (which indicates a cycle). The algorithm: 1. Mark current node as visited. 2. For each neighbor: a. If unvisited, recursively call DFS with current node as parent. If returns True, propagate True. b. If visited AND neighbor ≠ parent, return True (cycle detected). 3. Return False if no cycle found. Time complexity for this function is O(degree(node)) in isolation, but O(V+E) overall. Space is O(V) for recursion stack. This connects to L0 by being the core function called within the full graph traversal. Common mistakes: forgetting to pass parent parameter, incorrect parent comparison, or not propagating cycle detection upward. Real code pattern: def dfs(node, parent): visited[node]=True; for neighbor in graph[node]: if not visited[neighbor]: if dfs(neighbor, node): return True; elif neighbor != parent: return True; return False.

examples.input: Start node=0, parent=-1, graph: 0 connected to 1 and 2, 1 connected to 0 and 3, visited initially all False
examples.output: False (assuming no cycle in this subgraph)
examples.trace: DFS(0, -1): Mark 0 visited. Neighbors: 1 (unvisited) → DFS(1, 0): Mark 1 visited. Neighbors: 0 (visited, but parent=0, ignore), 3 (unvisited) → DFS(3, 1): Mark 3 visited. No further neighbors. Return False back to DFS(1). Next neighbor of 0: 2 (unvisited) → DFS(2, 0): Mark 2 visited. No cycle detected. Return False.

examples.input: Start node=0, parent=-1, graph: 0-1, 1-2, 2-0 (triangle), visited initially all False
examples.trace: DFS(0, -1): Mark 0 visited. Neighbors: 1 (unvisited) → DFS(1, 0): Mark 1 visited. Neighbors: 0 (visited, parent=0, ignore), 2 (unvisited) → DFS(2, 1): Mark 2 visited. Neighbors: 1 (visited, parent=1, ignore), 0 (visited AND neighbor=0 ≠ parent=1) → CYCLE DETECTED. Return True.

testCases.input: Node=0, parent=-1, graph: single node 0, no edges
Node=0, parent=-1, graph: 0-1, 1-0 (self-loop via two edges)
Node=1, parent=0, graph: 1 connected to 0 and 2, with 0 already visited
Node=2, parent=1, graph: 2 connected to 1 and 3, with 1 already visited, 3 unvisited
Node=2, parent=1, graph: 2 connected to 1 and 0, with both 0 and 1 visited, and 0 ≠ parent

ladders.title: Core Logic — Detecting Cycle via Visited Node Check with Parent Comparison
ladders.desc: The central condition: if a visited neighbor is not the parent, cycle exists.
ladders.explanation: This is the fundamental insight that makes the algorithm work. In undirected graph DFS, when we encounter a visited node, we must determine whether it represents a cycle. The key observation: in tree traversal (which is cycle-free), each node only revisits its immediate parent. So if we find a visited node that is NOT the parent, it means there's an alternative path to that node, creating a cycle. The logic: during DFS from node 'u' to neighbor 'v', if 'v' is already visited AND v ≠ parent[u], then we've found a back-edge that completes a cycle. The intuition: parent tracking creates a tree structure of the DFS traversal; any edge connecting a node to a non-parent visited node creates a cycle because it adds an extra connection outside the tree. This condition is checked for each edge exactly once due to undirected nature. Time complexity for this check is O(1) per edge. Space is O(1). This connects to L1 as the critical condition within the DFS function. Common mistakes: forgetting the 'visited' check, comparing with wrong parent, or applying to directed graphs. Real code pattern: if visited[neighbor] and neighbor != parent: return True.

examples.trace: Processing node 2 with parent 1. Mark 2 visited. Check neighbor 1: visited=True, but neighbor=1 equals parent=1 → ignore. Check neighbor 3: visited=False → explore recursively (not shown). Check neighbor 0: visited=True AND neighbor=0 ≠ parent=1 → CYCLE CONDITION TRIGGERED. Return True.

Processing node 1 with parent 0. Mark 1 visited. Check neighbor 0: visited=True, but neighbor=0 equals parent=0 → ignore. Check neighbor 2: visited=False → explore recursively (not shown). No cycle condition met. Return False.

testCases.input: Current node=0, parent=-1, neighbor=1, visited[1]=True (but 1 not parent)

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Traversal with Visited Tracking
ladders.desc: Performing DFS on a graph while maintaining visited array to avoid reprocessing.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves traversing a graph using Depth-First Search while tracking which nodes have been visited to prevent infinite loops and repeated processing. The intuition is that graphs can have cycles, so without visited tracking, traversal could get stuck in endless loops. The visited array (typically boolean) ensures each node is processed exactly once. For DFS, we start from a node, mark it visited, then recursively visit all unvisited neighbors. This builds a traversal tree. In disconnected graphs, we iterate through all nodes, starting DFS from each unvisited node. The time complexity is O(V+E) as each node and edge is processed once. Space is O(V) for visited array and recursion stack. This connects to L2 by providing the framework where parent comparison occurs during neighbor processing. Common mistakes: not resetting visited for new problems, using visited incorrectly in recursion, or forgetting disconnected components. Real code pattern: visited = [False]*V; def dfs(node): visited[node]=True; for neighbor in graph[node]: if not visited[neighbor]: dfs(neighbor).

examples.input: Graph with 4 vertices: edges: 0-1, 1-2, 2-3, start from node 0
examples.trace: Start DFS(0): Mark 0 visited. Neighbors: 1 (unvisited) → DFS(1): Mark 1 visited. Neighbors: 0 (visited, skip), 2 (unvisited) → DFS(2): Mark 2 visited. Neighbors: 1 (visited, skip), 3 (unvisited) → DFS(3): Mark 3 visited. Neighbors: 2 (visited, skip). Done.

examples.input: Graph with 3 vertices: edges: 0-1, 2 isolated, start from node 0
examples.output: Visited: 0,1 from first DFS; then 2 from second DFS
examples.trace: First, node 0 unvisited → DFS(0): Mark 0 visited. Neighbor: 1 (unvisited) → DFS(1): Mark 1 visited. Neighbors: 0 (visited, skip). Next, node 2 unvisited → DFS(2): Mark 2 visited. No neighbors. All nodes visited.

testCases.input: Graph: 1 vertex, no edges, start from 0

testCases.expected: Visited: 0,1 from first DFS; 2,3 from second
Visited: 0,1,2 from first DFS; 3,4,5 from second

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Through Graph Adjacency List
ladders.desc: Accessing and processing all neighbors of a node from adjacency list representation.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves working with the graph's adjacency list data structure. An adjacency list represents a graph as an array of lists, where each index corresponds to a vertex, and its list contains all adjacent vertices. The intuition is that this representation efficiently stores sparse graphs and allows quick access to neighbors. The basic operation is: for a given node u, iterate through graph[u] (its adjacency list) to access each neighbor v. This is typically done with a for-loop. In undirected graphs, each edge appears twice (once in each direction's list). This operation is O(degree(u)) time and O(1) space per node. It connects to L3 as the mechanism for exploring neighbors during traversal. Common mistakes: going out of bounds, modifying list during iteration, or misunderstanding directed vs undirected representation. Real code pattern: for neighbor in graph[node]: # process neighbor.

examples.trace: Iteration 1: neighbor=1 → process. Iteration 2: neighbor=2 → process. Iteration 3: neighbor=3 → process. Loop ends.
Iteration 1: neighbor=1 → process. Iteration 2: neighbor=3 → process. Done.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graph Representation and Basic Terminology
ladders.desc: Understanding what a graph is, vertices, edges, and adjacency list representation.
ladders.explanation: This is the foundational concept for beginners. A graph is a data structure consisting of vertices (nodes) and edges connecting them. In an undirected graph, edges have no direction (like two-way streets). A cycle is a path that starts and ends at the same vertex without repeating edges. The adjacency list is a common way to represent graphs: it's an array where each element is a list of neighboring vertices. For example, if vertex 0 is connected to vertices 1 and 2, then graph[0] = [1,2]. This representation is efficient for sparse graphs. The intuition is that it directly models connections. Key terms: vertex/node, edge, neighbor/adjacent, degree (number of edges). This connects to L4 as the data structure we iterate over. Common mistakes: confusing directed/undirected, misunderstanding zero-based indexing, or incorrect edge representation. Real code pattern: graph = [[] for _ in range(V)]; for edge in edges: graph[u].append(v); graph[v].append(u) # for undirected.

examples.trace: Start with empty lists for 3 vertices. Add edge 0-1: append 1 to graph[0], append 0 to graph[1]. Add edge 1-2: append 2 to graph[1], append 1 to graph[2]. Result: graph[0]=[1], graph[1]=[0,2], graph[2]=[1].

examples.output: Adjacency list: graph[0]=[1,2], graph[1]=[0], graph[2]=[0,3], graph[3]=[2]
examples.trace: Initialize 4 empty lists. Edge 0-1: graph[0]+=1, graph[1]+=0. Edge 0-2: graph[0]+=2, graph[2]+=0. Edge 2-3: graph[2]+=3, graph[3]+=2. Final lists as above.

approaches.name: Approach 3: Union-Find (Disjoint Set Union)
approaches.summary: Iterate through all edges, using union-find to merge sets of nodes. If two nodes of an edge are already in the same set, a cycle is detected due to redundant connection.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(E * α(V)) where α is the inverse Ackermann function

ladders.title: Full Problem — Detect Cycle in Undirected Graph Using Union-Find
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of cycle detection in an undirected graph using the Union-Find data structure.
ladders.explanation: The Union-Find approach detects cycles in undirected graphs by treating each node as a separate set and merging sets through edges. The intuition is that in an undirected graph, a cycle exists if we encounter an edge connecting two nodes that already belong to the same connected component. This happens because that edge would create a redundant connection, forming a closed loop. The algorithm initializes a parent array where each node is its own parent (representing separate sets). For each edge (u, v), we find the root (representative) of u and v using the find operation with path compression. If both roots are the same, the nodes are already connected, indicating a cycle. Otherwise, we union the two sets by making one root the parent of the other, using union by rank to optimize tree height. The process continues until a cycle is detected or all edges are processed. Time complexity is O(E * α(V)), where α is the inverse Ackermann function (effectively constant), and space is O(V) for parent and rank arrays. This connects to the broader problem by providing an efficient alternative to BFS/DFS for cycle detection. Common mistakes include not handling path compression correctly, forgetting to initialize ranks, or incorrectly implementing union by rank. Real code pattern involves find(x) with path compression and union(x, y) with rank optimization.

examples.trace: Initialize parent = [0,1,2,3], rank = [0,0,0,0]. Edge [0,1]: find(0)=0, find(1)=1, roots differ → union(0,1): parent[1]=0, rank[0]=1. Edge [1,2]: find(1)=0 (path: 1→0), find(2)=2, roots differ → union(0,2): parent[2]=0, rank[0]=1 (unchanged as ranks equal). Edge [2,3]: find(2)=0 (path: 2→0), find(3)=3, roots differ → union(0,3): parent[3]=0, rank[0]=1. Edge [3,0]: find(3)=0 (path: 3→0), find(0)=0, roots same → cycle detected, return True.

Initialize parent = [0,1,2], rank = [0,0,0]. Edge [0,1]: find(0)=0, find(1)=1, roots differ → union(0,1): parent[1]=0, rank[0]=1. Edge [1,2]: find(1)=0 (path: 1→0), find(2)=2, roots differ → union(0,2): parent[2]=0, rank[0]=1. All edges processed, no cycle → return False.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Union Operation with Rank Optimization
ladders.desc: Implement the union function that merges two sets while maintaining balanced trees using union by rank.
ladders.explanation: The union operation is crucial in Union-Find as it combines two disjoint sets into one. The intuition is to connect the roots of two sets, but doing so arbitrarily can lead to tall, unbalanced trees, degrading performance. Union by rank optimizes this by always attaching the shorter tree under the taller one, keeping the tree height minimal. This works because it ensures that the find operation remains efficient. The algorithm takes two nodes x and y, finds their roots using the find function (assumed implemented), and if roots differ, compares their ranks. The root with lower rank becomes the child of the higher-ranked root. If ranks are equal, arbitrarily choose one as parent and increment its rank by 1. This step-by-step process maintains balance: find root_x and root_y, if root_x != root_y, compare rank[root_x] and rank[root_y], attach lower rank to higher, update rank if equal. Time complexity is O(α(V)) due to find operations, space is O(1) extra. This feeds into the full problem by enabling efficient set merging during cycle detection. Common mistakes include not updating ranks correctly when equal, or modifying parent of non-root nodes. Real code pattern: if rank[root_x] < rank[root_y]: parent[root_x] = root_y; else: parent[root_y] = root_x; if ranks equal: increment one rank.

examples.trace: Find root of 0: 0. Find root of 1: 1. Roots differ (0 != 1). Compare rank[0]=0 and rank[1]=0: equal. Arbitrarily set parent[1]=0. Since ranks equal, increment rank[0] to 1. Result: parent = [0,0,2], rank = [1,0,0].

Find root of 0: 0 (path compression not shown). Find root of 2: 2. Roots differ (0 != 2). Compare rank[0]=1 and rank[2]=0: rank[0] > rank[2]. Set parent[2]=0. No rank change as higher rank attaches lower. Result: parent = [0,0,0], rank = [1,0,0].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Find Operation with Path Compression
ladders.desc: Implement the find function that locates the root of a set while flattening the tree for future efficiency.
ladders.explanation: The find operation is the heart of Union-Find, determining the representative (root) of a set for a given node. The intuition is that by recursively finding the parent until reaching the root, we can identify which set a node belongs to. Path compression optimizes this by making each node point directly to the root during the search, flattening the tree and ensuring near-constant time operations. This works because after compression, subsequent finds become faster. The algorithm takes a node x and checks if parent[x] equals x; if so, x is the root. Otherwise, recursively find the root of parent[x] and set parent[x] to that root before returning it. This step-by-step process: if parent[x] == x, return x; else, set parent[x] = find(parent[x]) and return parent[x]. Time complexity is O(α(V)) amortized due to compression, space is O(V) for recursion stack (or iterative implementation uses O(1) extra). This feeds into union and cycle detection by enabling efficient root queries. Common mistakes include not implementing recursion correctly, forgetting to update parent[x], or using iterative version without compression. Real code pattern: if parent[x] != x: parent[x] = find(parent[x]); return parent[x].

examples.trace: Start at node 3: parent[3] = 2, not root. Recursively find(2): parent[2] = 1, not root. Recursively find(1): parent[1] = 0, not root. Recursively find(0): parent[0] = 0, root found. Return 0 to find(1), set parent[1] = 0. Return 0 to find(2), set parent[2] = 0. Return 0 to find(3), set parent[3] = 0. Final parent = [0,0,0,0], return 0.
Start at node 0: parent[0] = 0, root found immediately. Return 0, no compression needed. Parent remains [0,1,2,3].

ladders.title: Building Block — Disjoint Set Representation with Parent Array
ladders.desc: Create and initialize the parent array that represents disjoint sets in Union-Find.
ladders.explanation: The parent array is the foundational data structure in Union-Find, representing each node's connection to its set. The intuition is that each set forms a tree where each node points to its parent, and the root points to itself, acting as the set representative. This works because it allows efficient traversal to find roots and merge sets. Initially, with no edges, each node is in its own set, so parent[i] = i for all nodes. The algorithm involves creating an array of size V (number of nodes) and setting each index to its own value. This step-by-step: initialize parent = list of size V, for i from 0 to V-1: parent[i] = i. Time complexity is O(V), space is O(V). This feeds into find and union operations by providing the initial state. Common mistakes include using wrong array size, not initializing all nodes, or misunderstanding that parent[i] = i means self-loop. Real code pattern: parent = [i for i in range(V)].

examples.trace: Create array of size 4. For i=0: set parent[0]=0. For i=1: set parent[1]=1. For i=2: set parent[2]=2. For i=3: set parent[3]=3. Result: parent = [0,1,2,3].
Create array of size 1. For i=0: set parent[0]=0. Result: parent = [0].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Initialization and Indexing
ladders.desc: Initialize an array and access its elements using indices, a fundamental operation in data structures.
ladders.explanation: Array initialization and indexing are basic programming operations essential for implementing data structures like the parent array in Union-Find. The intuition is that an array provides contiguous memory locations to store values, accessible via indices starting from 0. This works because it allows efficient O(1) access and update. To initialize an array of size n with values, create a list and assign values using a loop or list comprehension. For indexing, use array[index] to read or modify the value at that position. Step-by-step: for initialization, create an empty list or use [value] * n (if value is immutable), but for sequential values like in parent array, use a loop: array = [], for i in range(n): array.append(i). For indexing, to access element at index i, use array[i]; to update, set array[i] = new_value. Time complexity for initialization is O(n), for indexing O(1); space is O(n). This feeds into building the parent array by setting up the data structure. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in indices, misunderstanding zero-based indexing, or improper initialization for mutable values. Real code pattern: arr = [i for i in range(n)] for initialization, val = arr[idx] for access.

examples.input: Initialize array of size 3 with values 0,1,2
examples.trace: Create empty list. For i=0: append 0 → list = [0]. For i=1: append 1 → list = [0,1]. For i=2: append 2 → list = [0,1,2]. Result: [0,1,2].

examples.input: Array = [10,20,30]; access index 1, then set index 2 to 40
examples.trace: Start with array = [10,20,30]. Access index 1: array[1] = 20. Set index 2 to 40: array[2] = 40. Array becomes [10,20,40].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graph Representation as List of Edges
ladders.desc: Understand how an undirected graph can be represented using a list of edges, where each edge is a pair of nodes.
ladders.explanation: Graph representation as a list of edges is a simple way to model connections between nodes, fundamental for graph algorithms like cycle detection. The intuition is that a graph consists of nodes (vertices) and edges connecting them; in an undirected graph, edges have no direction, so an edge between node u and node v is symmetric. This works because it captures all relationships without extra structure. For example, a graph with nodes 0,1,2 and edges connecting 0-1 and 1-2 can be represented as [[0,1], [1,2]]. Each inner list [u,v] means an edge exists between u and v. Step-by-step: identify all nodes (usually from 0 to V-1), list each edge as a pair, ensure no duplicate edges unless specified. Time and space depend on implementation but generally O(E) to store edges. This feeds into cycle detection by providing the input to process edges. Common mistakes include confusing directed vs undirected edges, missing nodes, or incorrect pair ordering. Real code pattern: edges = [[0,1], [1,2]] for a graph.

examples.input: Graph with 3 nodes: edges between 0-1 and 1-2
examples.trace: Nodes are 0,1,2. Edge from 0 to 1: represent as [0,1]. Edge from 1 to 2: represent as [1,2]. List all edges: [[0,1], [1,2]].

examples.input: Graph with 2 nodes: single edge between 0 and 1
examples.trace: Nodes are 0,1. Edge from 0 to 1: represent as [0,1]. Only one edge, so list is [[0,1]].

testCases.input: Graph with 1 node and no edges
Graph with 3 nodes and single edge 1-2
